perturbation.original_text: “Affiliate” means any entity (including without limitation any individual, corporation, company, partnership, limited liability company or group) that directly, or indirectly through one or more intermediaries, controls, is controlled by or is under common control with such party.
perturbation.changed_text: “Affiliate” means any entity (including without limitation any corporation, company, partnership, limited liability company or group) that directly controls such party.
perturbation.explanation: The original definition of 'Affiliate' includes entities under indirect control or common control, while the modified definition omits these aspects, focusing solely on direct control. This creates an in-text contradiction because the scope of who is considered an affiliate is narrowed significantly. This is important because the contract later states  'Recipient agrees to be responsible for any breach of this Agreement by its Affiliates and its or their Authorized Parties.' , the narrowing of the definition would affect the legal obligations of the recipient.
perturbation.contradicted_text: The Recipient further agrees that prior to disclosing any Proprietary Information to its Affiliates, or its or their Authorized Parties, as allowed hereunder, the Receiving Party shall advise such Affiliates and/or Authorized Parties of the proprietary n ature of the Proprietary Information, and either: (x) direct them to abide by the terms of this Agreement; or (y) ensure that they are under written agreement with the Recipient that establishes non -disclosure and limited use restrictions regarding the Pro prietary Information that are no less restrictive than those set forth herein. The Recipient agrees to be responsible for any breach of this Agreement by its Affiliates and its or their Authorized Parties.
perturbation.justification: The original text's definition of 'Affiliate' included entities under indirect or common control, but the changed text narrows it to only entities under direct control. The contradicted text holds the Recipient responsible for breaches by its Affiliates. By narrowing the definition of Affiliate, the modified text reduces the Recipient's responsibility for breaches caused by entities that would have been considered affiliates under the original, broader definition but are no longer considered as such. Therefore, the changed text creates a direct contradiction with the contradicted text by limiting the scope of liability.

perturbation.original_text: “Authorized Parties” means directors, officers, employees, agents, advisors, representatives, service providers, consultants and/or sub -contractors.
perturbation.changed_text: “Authorized Parties” means only directors and officers.
perturbation.explanation: The initial definition of 'Authorized Parties' encompasses a wide range of individuals and entities, but the altered definition strictly limits this to directors and officers. This creates a contradiction since other sections might assume the broader original definition, particularly concerning liability and information disclosure. The 'Authorized Parties' are referenced in section 2.1 of the contract stating 'Curtiss -Wright and Company agree that, as a condition to the receipt of Proprietary Information hereunder, Recipient shall: (i) not disclose, directly or indire ctly, to any third party any portion of the Proprietary Information without the prior written consent of the Disclosing Party (except to its Affiliates, and its or their Authorized Parties who have a need to know the Proprietary Information and only to the extent necessary for the Purpose)'.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Curtiss -Wright and Company agree that, as a condition to the receipt of Proprietary Information hereunder, Recipient shall: (i) not disclose, directly or indire ctly, to any third party any portion of the Proprietary Information without the prior written consent of the Disclosing Party (except to its Affiliates, and its or their Authorized Parties who have a need to know the Proprietary Information and only to the extent necessary for the Purpose); (ii) not use the Proprietary Information except for the Purpose; (iii) promptly return or destroy, at the Disclosing Party’s request, all materials and documentation containing the Proprietary Information received hereun der except for copies of any computer records or electronic files containing the Proprietary Information, which have been created by automated processes such as archiving or back -up procedures; (iv) take all reasonably necessary precautions to protect the confidentiality of the Proprietary Information received hereunder and exercise at least the same degree of care in safeguarding the Proprietary Information as Recipient, its Affiliates, or its or their Authorized Parties would with its own proprietary info rmation; (v) inform all Authorized Parties of Recipient and its Affiliates to whom Proprietary Information may be disclosed or made available of the proprietary interest of the Disclosing Party therein and of the obligations of Recipient with respect there to; and (vi) promptly advise the Disclosing Party in writing upon learning of any unauthorized use or disclosure of the Proprietary Information.
perturbation.justification: The modified definition of 'Authorized Parties' significantly narrows its scope from a broad range of individuals/entities to only directors and officers. The contradicted text in section 2.1 explicitly mentions 'Authorized Parties' in the context of information disclosure obligations. The original definition allowed disclosure to a wider group, including consultants and subcontractors, but the changed definition restricts this to directors and officers only. This creates a direct contradiction because the recipient is obligated to inform 'all Authorized Parties' of confidentiality obligations, a task that is impossible if the definition has been narrowed and now excludes individuals previously considered 'Authorized Parties'. This incompatibility makes the enforcement of information disclosure unclear, fulfilling the Ambiguities - In Text Contradiction perturbation type requirements.

perturbation.original_text: “Proprietary Information” means confidential, non -public or other proprietary in formation including, without limitation, trade secrets, technical information, including algorithms, code, data, designs, documentation, drawings, formulae, hardware, software, know -how, ideas, inventions, whether patentable or not, photographs, recordings , procedures, processes, reports, research, samples, sketches, specifications, operations, plans, products, financial information, including pricing and other proprietary information that is disclosed under the terms of this Agreement by Disclosing Party o r its Affiliates to the Recipient.
perturbation.changed_text: “Proprietary Information” means only trade secrets explicitly marked as such and provided in hard copy format.
perturbation.explanation: The original definition of 'Proprietary Information' is broad, including various types of confidential information. The modified definition is very narrow, limited to trade secrets in hard copy. This creates a strong contradiction. This will affect section 2.1 of the agreement because the limited 'Proprietary Information' will be applied.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Curtiss -Wright and Company agree that, as a condition to the receipt of Proprietary Information hereunder, Recipient shall: (i) not disclose, directly or indire ctly, to any third party any portion of the Proprietary Information without the prior written consent of the Disclosing Party (except to its Affiliates, and its or their Authorized Parties who have a need to know the Proprietary Information and only to the extent necessary for the Purpose); (ii) not use the Proprietary Information except for the Purpose; (iii) promptly return or destroy, at the Disclosing Party’s request, all materials and documentation containing the Proprietary Information received hereun der except for copies of any computer records or electronic files containing the Proprietary Information, which have been created by automated processes such as archiving or back -up procedures; (iv) take all reasonably necessary precautions to protect the confidentiality of the Proprietary Information received hereunder and exercise at least the same degree of care in safeguarding the Proprietary Information as Recipient, its Affiliates, or its or their Authorized Parties would with its own proprietary info rmation; (v) inform all Authorized Parties of Recipient and its Affiliates to whom Proprietary Information may be disclosed or made available of the proprietary interest of the Disclosing Party therein and of the obligations of Recipient with respect there to; and (vi) promptly advise the Disclosing Party in writing upon learning of any unauthorized use or disclosure of the Proprietary Information.
perturbation.justification: The original text defines 'Proprietary Information' broadly, whereas the changed text limits it to only 'trade secrets explicitly marked as such and provided in hard copy format.' This creates a direct contradiction with the contradicted text, which imposes obligations on the recipient of 'Proprietary Information' including not disclosing it, protecting its confidentiality, and returning or destroying materials containing it. The narrowed definition in the changed text means that much of what was originally considered 'Proprietary Information' is no longer covered, thus diminishing the scope of the recipient's obligations outlined in the contradicted text. This falls under the Ambiguities - In Text Contradiction perturbation type.

perturbation.original_text: 2.4. This Agreement shall be effective as of the Effective Date below and shall expire three (3) years after the Effective Date. Either party may terminate this Agreement without cause by giving thirty (30) days prior written notice of termination to the other party. The obligation of confidentia lity and non -disclosure with respect to Proprietary Information shall expire on the later of the date: (i) that is five (5) years from the Effective Date below; or (ii) when the Proprietary Information is no longer protected as a trade secret under applica ble law.
perturbation.changed_text: 2.4. This Agreement shall be effective as of the Effective Date below and shall expire one (1) year after the Effective Date. Either party may terminate this Agreement without cause by giving thirty (30) days prior written notice of termination to the other party. The obligation of confidentia lity and non -disclosure with respect to Proprietary Information shall expire on the later of the date: (i) that is five (5) years from the Effective Date below; or (ii) when the Proprietary Information is no longer protected as a trade secret under applica ble law.
perturbation.explanation: The modification changes the Agreement expiration from 'three (3) years' to 'one (1) year'. However, the obligations of confidentiality extends for 5 years after the effective date of the agreement. This contradiction creates confusion regarding the duration of the agreement versus the duration of confidentiality.
perturbation.contradicted_text: This Agreement shall be effective as of the Effective Date below and shall expire one (1) year after the Effective Date. Either party may terminate this Agreement without cause by giving thirty (30) days prior written notice of termination to the other party. The obligation of confidentia lity and non -disclosure with respect to Proprietary Information shall expire on the later of the date: (i) that is five (5) years from the Effective Date below; or (ii) when the Proprietary Information is no longer protected as a trade secret under applica ble law.
perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies the agreement expiration from three years to one year. The contradicted text, which is the same as the changed text, specifies that the confidentiality obligations extend for five years after the effective date. This creates a direct contradiction because the agreement's term is one year, but the confidentiality obligations last for five years, creating uncertainty about the parties' obligations during the period after the agreement has expired but the confidentiality obligations remain in effect. This aligns with the definition of 'Ambiguities - In Text Contradiction'.

perturbation.original_text: NEITHER PARTY MAKES ANY WARRANTIES REGARDING ITS PROPRIETARY INFORMATION AND THE PROPRIETARY INFORMATION IS PROVIDED “AS IS”. EACH PARTY DISCLAIMS ALL EXPRESS OR IMPLI ED WARRANTIES WITH RESPECT TO ITS PROPRIETARY INFORMATION, INCLUDING, WITHOUT LIMITATION, ALL IMPLIED WARRANTIES OF MERCHANTABILITY AND FITNESS FOR A PARTICULAR PURPOSE AND THE WARRANTY OF NON -INFRINGEMENT.
perturbation.changed_text: NEITHER PARTY MAKES ANY WARRANTIES REGARDING ITS PROPRIETARY INFORMATION AND THE PROPRIETARY INFORMATION IS PROVIDED “AS IS”. EACH PARTY DISCLAIMS ALL EXPRESS OR IMPLI ED WARRANTIES WITH RESPECT TO ITS PROPRIETARY INFORMATION, INCLUDING, WITHOUT LIMITATION, ALL IMPLIED WARRANTIES OF MERCHANTABILITY AND FITNESS FOR A PARTICULAR PURPOSE AND THE WARRANTY OF NON -INFRINGEMENT. However, Disclosing Party warrants that it has the right to disclose the Proprietary Information.
perturbation.explanation: This change introduces a contradiction by initially disclaiming all warranties, including express warranties, and then introducing a specific warranty regarding the right to disclose Proprietary Information. This creates an in-text contradiction, since it's first stating 'NEITHER PARTY MAKES ANY WARRANTIES' but later, the Disclosing Party is warranting that it has the right to disclose the Proprietary Information.
perturbation.contradicted_text: The Disclosing Party retains all right, title and interest in and to its Proprietary Information and, except as provided herein, no lice nse or other right, express or implied is hereby transferred to the Receiving Party, including any license by implication, estoppel or otherwise, under any copyrights, mask works, trademarks, trade secrets, patents or other proprietary rights now held by, or which may be obtained by, or which is or may be licensable by the Disclosing Party. NEITHER PARTY MAKES ANY WARRANTIES REGARDING ITS PROPRIETARY INFORMATION AND THE PROPRIETARY INFORMATION IS PROVIDED “AS IS”. EACH PARTY DISCLAIMS ALL EXPRESS OR IMPLI ED WARRANTIES WITH RESPECT TO ITS PROPRIETARY INFORMATION, INCLUDING, WITHOUT LIMITATION, ALL IMPLIED WARRANTIES OF MERCHANTABILITY AND FITNESS FOR A PARTICULAR PURPOSE AND THE WARRANTY OF NON -INFRINGEMENT. However, Disclosing Party warrants that it has the right to disclose the Proprietary Information.
perturbation.justification: The original text and the contradicted text include a blanket disclaimer of all warranties regarding proprietary information. The changed text adds an express warranty by the Disclosing Party regarding the right to disclose Proprietary Information. This directly contradicts the initial disclaimer of 'NEITHER PARTY MAKES ANY WARRANTIES' creating a strong in-text contradiction as it's making a specific warranty after disclaiming all warranties.
